abstract: Serotonin (5-HT) is a central neuromodulator which is implicated in, amongst other functions, cognitive flexibility. 5-HT is released from the dorsal raphe nucleus (DRN) throughout nearly the entire forebrain. Little is known, however, about how serotonin affects downstream populations of neurons and how this modulation might support its cognitive functions. Here, we optogenetically stimulated serotonergic neurons in the DRN while recording large parts of the brain with Neuropixels during quiet wakefulness and performance of a perceptual decision-making task. During quiet wakefulness, 5-HT stimulation induced a rapid switch in internal state, as indicated by dilation of the pupil, suppression of hippocampal sharp wave ripples, and increased exploratory behaviors, such as whisking. To elucidate the brain-wide effect of serotonin release we performed acute Neuropixel recordings in seven target locations, a total of 7,478 neurons were recorded across 17 mice. We found that 5-HT stimulation significantly modulated neural activity in all the recorded brain regions, both during quiet wakefulness and task performance. During task performance, however, we observed no change in behavior when stimulating 5-HT. We found that the 5-HT modulation of high-dimensional neural dynamics is confined to a subspace which is orthogonal relative to the choice axis. These observations describe a possible mechanism for the induction of state-dependent stimulus representations, suggesting a neural basis for neuromodulatory effects on brain-wide circuits to flexible decision-making.

sentences.text: Serotonin (5-HT) is a central neuromodulator which is implicated in, amongst other functions, cognitive flexibility.
sentences.note: General serotonin / cognitive-flexibility framing; sets up the question but is not itself a claim of this paper.

sentences.text: 5-HT is released from the dorsal raphe nucleus (DRN) throughout nearly the entire forebrain.
sentences.note: Anatomical background motivating the brain-wide recording strategy; not a claim of this paper.

sentences.text: Little is known, however, about how serotonin affects downstream populations of neurons and how this modulation might support its cognitive functions.
sentences.note: Gap statement; motivates the study but not itself a claim.

sentences.text: Here, we optogenetically stimulated serotonergic neurons in the DRN while recording large parts of the brain with Neuropixels during quiet wakefulness and performance of a perceptual decision-making task.
sentences.note: Methods/scope sentence — names the SERT-Cre + ChR2 protocol and the brain-wide Neuropixels strategy. Maps to the dataset-scope claim and the optogenetic-protocol scope claim.

sentences.text: During quiet wakefulness, 5-HT stimulation induced a rapid switch in internal state, as indicated by dilation of the pupil, suppression of hippocampal sharp wave ripples, and increased exploratory behaviors, such as whisking.
sentences.note: The state-switch interpretive claim plus its three converging empirical readouts (pupil, SWR, exploratory behavior) all bundled into one sentence — mapped to the hypothesis plus its three supporting empirical claims.

sentences.text: To elucidate the brain-wide effect of serotonin release we performed acute Neuropixel recordings in seven target locations, a total of 7,478 neurons were recorded across 17 mice.
sentences.note: Direct scope statement of the dataset bounds — 7,478 neurons, 17 mice, 7 target trajectories.

sentences.text: We found that 5-HT stimulation significantly modulated neural activity in all the recorded brain regions, both during quiet wakefulness and task performance.
sentences.note: Brain-wide modulation claim plus the implicit acknowledgement that modulation is present in both contexts (the per-context strength comparison is captured by the weaker-during-task claim). The bidirectional aspect is implicit in the brain-wide claim itself; the abstract sentence understates this by saying 'modulated' without specifying sign heterogeneity.

sentences.text: During task performance, however, we observed no change in behavior when stimulating 5-HT.
sentences.note: Direct map to the behavioral null result. The abstract presents this as a flat empirical claim; the graph carries the additional structure that this null *refutes* the surprise hypothesis the paper began with.

sentences.text: We found that the 5-HT modulation of high-dimensional neural dynamics is confined to a subspace which is orthogonal relative to the choice axis.
sentences.note: The central empirical orthogonality finding plus its hypothetical reading. The abstract conflates the geometric finding (5ht-axis-orthogonal-to-choice-axis) with the broader hypothesis (hypothesis-orthogonal-neuromodulatory-subspace) — the graph separates them. Neither the per-region significance (orthogonality-significant-in-mpfc-vis-m2) nor the temporal-growth aspect is mentioned.

sentences.text: These observations describe a possible mechanism for the induction of state-dependent stimulus representations, suggesting a neural basis for neuromodulatory effects on brain-wide circuits to flexible decision-making.
sentences.note: Discussion-level interpretive sentence projecting orthogonality forward as a mechanism for state-dependent representation. Maps back to the orthogonal-subspace hypothesis as its forward-looking implication.
